coredata.dc:title: Sulfuric-acid-mediated synthesis strategy for multi-colour aggregation-induced emission fluorescent carbon dots: Application in anti-counterfeiting, information encryption, and rapid cytoplasmic imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Lijuan
Liu, Shaochi
Quan, Tian
Tao, Yongqing
Tian, Meng
Wang, Luchun
Wang, Junji
Wang, Dandan
Gao, Die
coredata.dc:description: Aggregation-induced emission fluorescent carbon dots (AIE-CDs) have applications in the fields of multi-colour anti-counterfeiting, information encryption, and imaging. In this study, four AIE-CDs (B-AIE-CDs, G-AIE-CDs, Y-AIE-CDs, and O-AIE-CDs) with blue, green, yellow, and orange fluorescence at high concentrations were fabricated using crystal violet as a precursor, solutions with different sulfuric acid concentrations as solvents under different temperatures and reaction times for the first time. The structural properties and fluorescence behaviour of the AIE-CDs were investigated. The results revealed that the sulfuric acid concentration had a significant effect on the fluorescence colour of the AIE-CDs because sulfuric acid can affect the degree of carbonisation, the type and content of nitrogen. Moreover, the reaction temperature and time affected the surface-defect state and the degree of carbonisation of the AIE-CDs, which affected the emission wavelength and quantum yield (QY) of the AIE-CDs. Furthermore, to exploit the unique characteristics (polychromatic aggregation fluorescence and acid-sensitive properties) of the obtained-AIE-CDs, anti-counterfeiting and information encryption methodologies (i.e., acid-stimuli-response producing multi-colour fluorescence) were preliminarily developed. Finally, B-AIE-CDs with a high QY of 43.5% were successfully used for rapid cytoplasmic imaging, demonstrating their applicability in biological fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aggregation-induced emission (AIE)
Carbon dots
Sulfuric acid-mediated
Multi-colour
Anti-counterfeiting information encryption
Cytoplasmic imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation process of the multi-colour emission fluorescent AIE-CDs
Steps to study the mechanism of endocytosis
Synthetic strategy of multi-colour emission fluorescent AIE-CDs
Structural and morphological characterizations of AIE-CDs
Optical performance and AIE properties of AIE-CDs
Application of AIE-CDs for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption
Application of AIE-CDs for information encryption
Application of B-AIE-CDs for rapid cytoplasmic imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A-D. HRTEM images of B-AIE-CDs (A), G-AIE-CDs (B), Y-AIE-CDs (C) and O-AIE-CDs (D). E-H. TEM images of B-AIE-CDs (E), G-AIE-CDs (F), Y-AIE-CDs (G) and O-AIE-CDs (H). (Inset were the diameter distributions of AIE-CDs calculated from TEM image).

A-D. XPS spectra of B-AIE-CDs (A), G-AIE-CDs (B), Y-AIE-CDs (C) and O-AIE-CDs E. The FT-IR spectra of four kinds of AIE-CDs and crystal violet. F. The FT-IR spectra of CDs which prepared under different reaction time. G. The FT-IR spectra of CDs which prepared under different reaction temperature.
A-B. UV–vis absorption spectra of our kinds of AIE-CDs and crystal violet. C-F. Bandgap energies of B-AIE-CDs (C), G-AIE-CDs (D), Y-AIE-CDs (E) and O-AIE-CDs (F).
Fluorescence emission spectra of B-AIE-CDs (A), G-AIE-CDs (B), Y-AIE-CDs (C) and O-AIE-CDs (D) with different excitation wavelengths.

A-D. AIE properties of B-AIE-CDs (A), G-AIE-CDs (B), Y-AIE-CDs (C) and O-AIE-CDs (D) under different concentrations under sunlight and 365 nm UV lamp. E-G. The emission wavelengths of O-AIE-CDs under the concentrations of 1 mg·mL−1 (E), 0.1 mg·mL−1 (F) and 0.01 mg·mL−1 (G).

A-B. Photographs of the anti-counterfeiting which writing by three kinds of AIE-CDs fluorescent inks under sunlight and under 365 nm UV lamp. C-D. Photographs of the anti-counterfeiting which writing by three kinds of AIE-CDs fluorescent inks under sunlight after being wet with sulfuric acid solution (pH = 1) and under 365 nm UV lamp after being wet with sulfuric acid solution (pH = 1). E-F. Photographs of the information encryption which drawing by Y-AIE-CDs and O-AIE-CDs fluorescent inks under sunlight and under 365 nm UV lamp. G-H. Photographs of the information encryption which drawing by Y-AIE-CDs and O-AIE-CDs fluorescent inks under sunlight after being wet with sulfuric acid solution (pH = 1) and under 365 nm UV lamp after being wet with sulfuric acid solution (pH = 1).

A. The fluorescent images of cells treated with 50 μg·mL-1B-AIE-CDs at different times (3–480 min). B. The fluorescent images of cell imaging under different concentrations (5–200 μg·mL−1) of B-AIE-CDs.
A. The image of cell nuclear imaging by using DAPI. B. The image of cytoplasmic imaging by using B-AIE-CDs. C. The merged images of the corresponding (A) and (B).

A. The fluorescent images of cells incubated with 50 μg·mL−1 of B-AIE-CDs (control). B-C. The fluorescent images of cells incubated with 50 μg·mL−1 of B-AIE-CDs under the conditions of 4 °C and NaN3. D-G. The fluorescent images of cells incubated with different endocytosis inhibitors.
Preparation processed of four kinds of AIE-CDs.
The process of the B-AIE-CDs for rapid cytoplasmic imaging through the clathrin-mediated endocytosis.
Optimization of the synthesis conditions of four kinds of AIE-CDs.
Elemental analysis of four kinds of AIE-CDs.

head.ce:title: Sulfuric-acid-mediated synthesis strategy for multi-colour aggregation-induced emission fluorescent carbon dots: Application in anti-counterfeiting, information encryption, and rapid cytoplasmic imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan, 646000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan, 646000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Four colors of AIE-CDs were successfully prepared by using crystal violet as precursor, different concentrations of sulfuric acid solution as the solvent under different temperature and reaction time. These AIE-CDs have unique advantages that they were successfully applied in the fields of multi-color anti-counterfeiting, information encryption and rapid cytoplasmic imaging.

Aggregation-induced emission fluorescent carbon dots (AIE-CDs) have applications in the fields of multi-colour anti-counterfeiting, information encryption, and imaging. In this study, four AIE-CDs (B-AIE-CDs, G-AIE-CDs, Y-AIE-CDs, and O-AIE-CDs) with blue, green, yellow, and orange fluorescence at high concentrations were fabricated using crystal violet as a precursor, solutions with different sulfuric acid concentrations as solvents under different temperatures and reaction times for the first time. The structural properties and fluorescence behaviour of the AIE-CDs were investigated. The results revealed that the sulfuric acid concentration had a significant effect on the fluorescence colour of the AIE-CDs because sulfuric acid can affect the degree of carbonisation, the type and content of nitrogen. Moreover, the reaction temperature and time affected the surface-defect state and the degree of carbonisation of the AIE-CDs, which affected the emission wavelength and quantum yield (QY) of the AIE-CDs. Furthermore, to exploit the unique characteristics (polychromatic aggregation fluorescence and acid-sensitive properties) of the obtained-AIE-CDs, anti-counterfeiting and information encryption methodologies (i.e., acid-stimuli-response producing multi-colour fluorescence) were preliminarily developed. Finally, B-AIE-CDs with a high QY of 43.5% were successfully used for rapid cytoplasmic imaging, demonstrating their applicability in biological fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a type of photoluminescence nanomaterial, have excellent optical properties, low toxicity, high biocompatibility, tunable surface characteristics, and significant photobleaching resistance [1,2]. Owing to these excellent properties, CDs have been widely used in various fields, such as sensing, bio-imaging, drug delivery, anti-counterfeiting, and information encryption [3–7]. Recently, the fluorescence colour of CDs prepared with the same carbon source is relatively simple, and most of the colours are concentrated in the range of blue to green [8]. This greatly limits the use of CDs, especially in multi-coloured light-emitting diodes (LEDs), anti-counterfeiting, information encryption, and imaging [9–11]. Moreover, when CDs are used for LEDs or anti-counterfeiting, they often need to be in a solid (powder) or aggregated state. Nevertheless, similar to organic fluorophores, the fluorescence of most CDs is quenched because of aggregation at high concentrations in the solution, solid, or powder states due to π-π stacking [12]. To stabilise the fluorescence of CDs and inhibit aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching, researchers typically use substrates to fix CDs, including the use of membranes and gels [13,14]. However, these methods cannot overcome aggregation-induced quenching. In addition, the preparation of these gels or films is complicated and time-consuming. Thus, it is of great significance to synthesise tunable multi-colour emission fluorescent CDs, especially aggregation-induced emission fluorescent carbon dots (AIE-CDs), as they can be effectively used in many areas.

The synthesis of multi-colour emission fluorescent AIE-CDs with high performance is challenging. As reported, the formation process and fluorescence mechanism of CDs are still controversial, and there is nounified method for obtaining long-wavelength emission CDs [8]. Most multi-colour CDs are synthesised by doping heteroatoms using a different solvent or carbon source, passivation agent, temperature, and reaction time [15,16]. The selection of the heteroatom precursors and solvent-mediated doping (such as introducing sulfuric acid and phosphoric acid) are common methods for preparing multi-coloured CDs. This is because they can lead to defects within the framework of the CDs, resulting in the contraction of the band gaps of the CDs and a redshift of the CD fluorescence emission wavelength [17,18]. To date, different heteroatoms, such as N and S, have been successfully doped into the structure of CDs to produce CDs with different colours [19,20]. In addition, controlling the extent of graphitisation, the number of surface functional groups on the CDs, and the heteroatomic content during doping by controlling the reaction temperature (thermal-pyrolysis temperature), reactant ratio, and reaction time can effectively lead to the generation of multi-colour-emission fluorescent CDs [21,22]. Acid-reagent-engineering-strategy-mediated tuning has also been reported as an efficient method for the preparation of multi-colour emission fluorescent CDs. Yang et al. proposed that acid-mediated fluorescence behaviour can be obtained by changing the pH of the hydrothermal precursors [23]. Furthermore, Wang et al. [24] fabricated a series of CDs with full-colour fluorescence rangingfrom blue to red, and even white, using o-phenylenediamine coupled with a series of acids. They found that acids can be effectively used to modulate the optical properties of CDs by adjusting the mass ratio between the precursor and acids. The increasing number of electron-withdrawing groups on the surface of CDs that originate from acid reagents boost the redshift of their fluorescence wavelength and increase their particle size. These results indicate that the colour of CDs can be adjusted by changing the strength or type of acid used. However, most of the studies mentioned above focused on studying a single factor that affects the fluorescence colour of CDs. Very few studies have focused on discussing the influence of multiple factors, such as the amount of acid used, reaction temperature and time, amount of doping heteroatoms, change of surface groups, and fluorescence characteristics of CDs. The preparation of multi-colour emission fluorescent CDs can be simplified by understanding the interactions of various factors. In addition, most of the previously reported CDs do not have AIE features; therefore, it is necessary to further explore the preparation methods of multi-colour emission fluorescent AIE-CDs.

Tang et al. suggested that typical AIE compounds, such as tetraphenylethane (TPE) and hexaphenylsilole, possess propeller-shaped structures with rotatable peripheral phenyl rings. AIE occurs through the restriction of intramolecular motions, including rotations and vibrations [25]. Most polymers prepared from these compounds exhibit AIE properties. Although CDs partially carbonise the precursor, they still contain some groups and polymers of the original precursor on their surface. Thus, CDs may exhibit AIE features if the precursor has features similar to typical AIE compounds.

Crystal violet (purity > 99%), quinine sulfate (>99%) was bought from Acros Co. Ltd. Rhodamine 6G (>99%), methyl-β-cyclodextrin (MβCD, >98%) was gained from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. NaN3 (>99%) was gained from Croto Biotechnology co. Ltd. DAPI, chlorpromazine (CPZ, >98%) and amiloride were gained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd., Genistein (>98%) was obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. Melanoma cells were obtained from the Cell Center of the Chinese Academy of Medicinal Sciences. 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT, >98%) and penicillin–streptomycin were purchased from Beijing Ding guo biotechnology co. Ltd. Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS) and trypsin were obtained from Hyclone. All aqueous solutions were made with deionized water. related to ions were obtained from Kelong Chemical Co. Ltd.

TEM images of AIE-CDs were obtained by using a transmission electron microscope with a model of JEOL 2100F (JEOL). The FT-IR spectra of AIE-CDs were acquired by an IR Affinity-1S FT-IR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). UV-2600 spectrometer (Shimadzu) was applied to obtain the UV–vis absorption spectra of AIE-CDs. The elements analysis of AIE-CDs were conducted on a K-Alpha+ X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo fisher Scientific). A LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer was used to explore the fluorescence properties of NCDs (Perkin Elmer). The fluorescence images were recorded using a IX-73 inverted fluorescence microscope (Olympus). MTT assay was completed on a synergy H1 microplate reader (Biotek).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation process of the multi-colour emission fluorescent AIE-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A simple bottom-up solvothermal method was used to synthesise multi-colour emission fluorescent AIE-CDs using crystal violet as the precursor and water solutions with different amounts of sulfuric acid as the solvent. Crystal violet (0.5 g) was first added to 15 mL of a sulfuric acid–water solution. Thereafter, the mixture was placed into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave for further reaction at different temperatures (200 °C, 220 °C, and 240 °C) for different durations (2 h, 6 h, and 12 h). After cooling, the dark brown solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove the insoluble precipitate. Next, the solution was filtered through a membrane filter (0.22 μm) and the AIE-CDs were neutralised with a sodium hydroxide solution. The solution was dialysed through a dialysis membrane (1000-Da tubing) for seven days to remove small molecules. Finally, the AIE-CDs were freeze-dried to obtain a dark brown powder. The preparation of the AIE-CDs is shown in Scheme 1 . The quantum yields (QYs) of AIE-CDs by using different reaction conditions were calculated using quinine sulfate (0.1 mol·L−1, QY = 54%) and rhodamine 6 G (ethanol solution, QY = 95%) as references (the excitation wavelengths were 360 nm and 480 nm, respectively). The details are provided in the Supporting Information .

AIE-CD powders were used as carbon ink, and a clean filter paper of an appropriate size was used for drawing. Assuming that the letters “S” and “U” in SWMU (abbreviation for Southwest Medical University) were the real password letters, and “W” and M“ were the false ones, we wrote ”S“ and ”U“ with O-AIE-CDs, while G-AIE-CDs and Y-AIE-CDs for the other two letters, respectively. Furthermore, a red marker pen was used to redraw once. The anti-counterfeiting string could be done with sulfuric acid–water solution (pH = 1) along the handwriting after being wetted and observed under a 365 UV lamp to view the real password letters, ”S“ and ”U“.

AIE-CDs powders were used as carbon ink, and a clean filter paper of an appropriate size was used for drawing. A simple stick figure containing the encrypted information needed, such as the number “123456789″, was written by using the O-AIE-CDs, G-AIE-CDs and Y-AIE-CDs for the necessary and unnecessary information, respectively. Subsequently, the stick figure was drawn again using a black marker pen. After wetting the drawing with sulfuric acid–water solution (pH = 1) along the painting trace, we checked the encrypted message ”123456789″ under the 365 UV lamp.

MTT measurements were conducted on melanoma cells to explore the biocompatibility and cytotoxicity of B-AIE-CDs (CDs with high QY were used for this investigation). The melanoma cells were first seeded in 96-well plates and incubated in a CO2 incubator for 24 h. Thereafter, different concentrations (2.5–1000 μg·mL−1) of B-AIE-CDs were added to the wells and incubated for an additional 24 h. Next, 10 μL of MTT (5.0 mg·mL−1) was added to each well and incubated for 4 h. The solutions in all the wells were removed, and 150 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to each well to dissolve the formazan complex. After shaking the plate for 10 min, the absorbance was measured at 570 nm using a Synergy H1 microplate reader.

The melanoma cells were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) in six-well plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. The cells were then treated with B-AIE-CDs at different concentrations (5 μg·mL−1 to 200 μg·mL−1) for different durations (3 min, 5 min, 10 min, 15 min, 30 min, 60 min, 120 min, 240 min, and 480 min). Next, the DMEM was removed, and melanoma cells were washed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) (10 mM, pH = 7.4) thrice and kept in PBS for further fluorescence imaging. In the colocalisation experiments, melanoma cells were incubated with B-AIE-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) for 5 min. The cells were then treated with the commercial dye, DAPI (0.03 mM), for 5 min. Next, the cells were washed with PBS (10 mM, pH = 7.4) thrice and then incubated in PBS for further fluorescence imaging. All images were captured using an inverted fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steps to study the mechanism of endocytosis
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the endocytosis mechanism of B-AIE-CDs, the melanoma cells were separately pre-treated at low temperature (4 °C) with NaN3 (10 mM), 10 μg·mL−1 of CPZ, 100 μg·mL−1 of genistein, 10 μg·mL−1 of MβCD, or 20 μg·mL−1 of amiloride for 4 h. Next, the cells were incubated with 50 μg·mL−1 of B-AIE-CDs for 5 min and washed with PBS (10 mM, pH = 7.4) thrice. Finally, fluorescence imaging was performed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthetic strategy of multi-colour emission fluorescent AIE-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of AIE-CDs is shown in Scheme 1. To the best of our knowledge, crystal violet, also known as methyl violet, is a common triphenylmethane dye with a highly symmetric conjugated structure and rich nitrogen content. It can act as both a C and N source in the reaction. Moreover, the water solution, with a certain amount of sulfuric acid as the reaction solvent, can release a large amount of heat, promote the dehydration and carbonisation processes, and dope a small amount of S in the structure of the prepared-CDs. In addition, the reaction temperature and time also have an effect on the graphitisation and the number of surface functional groups on the CDs, which affects the fluorescent colour of the CDs. Thus, to obtain multi-colour emission fluorescent AIE-CDs, the effect of the amount of sulfuric acid, reaction temperature, and reaction time on the formation of CDs was systematically investigated.

The growth processes of AIE-CDs included the following stages: under the action of concentrated sulfuric acid, some macromolecules and long-chain polymers were formed due to oxidation and dehydration by intermolecular and intramolecular reactions; subsequently, the crystal nuclei of the AIE-CDs were formed. Next, under solvothermal conditions, the degree of carbonisation of the AIE-CDs increased, and the crystal nuclei gradually grew. Intermolecular oxidation, dehydration, and carbonisation rendered the surface of the AIE-CDs with an abundant amount of chemical groups (such as amino and hydroxyl groups) [26,27].

Herein, the effects of the amount of sulfuric acid, reaction temperature, and reaction time on the emission wavelength of the prepared AIE-CDs were investigated using single-factor experiments. As the amount of sulfuric acid increased, the reaction time and temperature decreased, and the emission wavelength of the CDs became more redshifted (shown in Table 1 ). Finally, the O-AIE-CDs with orange fluorescence were obtained under the synthetic conditions of 7 mL sulfuric acid, 200 °C and 2 h. Y-AIE-CDs (yellow fluorescence) and G-AIE-CDs (green fluorescence) were prepared separately using 5 mL and 3 mL of sulfuric acid, respectively, at 200 °C for 2 h. The B-AIE-CDs with blue fluorescence could be obtained by using 0 mL of sulfuric acid at 240 °C for 12 h. These results indicate that varying the amount of sulfuric acid, reaction temperature, and reaction time varied the degree of carbonisation of CDs; the types, rates, and degrees of intermolecular and intramolecular reactions; the degree of defects; and nitrogen doping on the surface of CDs, thereby affecting the fluorescence characteristics [28]. Furthermore, the QYs of B-AIE-CDs, G-AIE-CDs, Y-AIE-CDs, and O-AIE-CDs were calculated as 43.5%, 5.2%, 2.7%, and 1.3%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characterizations of AIE-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The morphologies of the B-AIE-CDs, G-AIE-CDs, Y-AIE-CDs, and O-AIE-CDs were observed using TEM and high-resolution transmission emission microscopy (HRTEM). It can be seen from Fig. 1
A-D that these AIE-CDs were uniform and monodisperse nanodots. The average lateral sizes were approximately 2.0 nm, 2.7 nm, 3.5 nm, and 3.5 nm for B-AIE-CDs, G-AIE-CDs, Y-AIE-CDs, and O-AIE-CDs, respectively. The HRTEM images illustrate that the four types of AIE-CDs had a highly crystalline structure with similar well-resolved lattice fringes. Lattice-fringe spacings of 0.23, 0.22, 0.22, and 0.21 nm which were assigned to the in-plane lattice spacing of the (100) facet of graphite were observed (Fig. 1 E-1H) [29–31]. The sizes of the B-AIE-CDs, G-AIE-CDs, Y-AIE-CDs, and O-AIE-CDs gradually increased while the lattice spacing gradually decreased, indicating that the degree of carbonisation of the AIE-CDs increased with the addition of different amounts of sulfuric acid. The higher degree of carbonisation degree was associated with a better graphitisation effect and a higher degree of conjugated structure (including the sub-domain with larger size and the molecular state-related structure with larger conjugation); therefore, the band gap narrowed and a redshift emission occurred. These results indicate that the size effect caused by the different degrees of carbonisation was one of the reasons for tuning the fluorescence wavelength [26].

XPS analysis was performed to analyse the elements of the prepared AIE-CDs. As seen in Fig. 2
A-D and
Figure S2 , all the full spectra of the AIE-CDs showed three typical peaks: C1s (284.8 eV), N1s (400.8 eV), and O1s (532.2 eV). In addition, the spectra of the G-AIE-CDs, Y-AIE-CDs, and O-AIE-CDs also showed S2p peaks. These results indicate that S was successfully doped into the structures of these three types of AIE-CDs. Moreover, it is apparent that all the C1s bands of AIE-CDs can be deconvoluted into three peaks corresponding to CC/CC (284.7 eV), CN/CS (285.4 eV), and carboxylic CO (286.3 eV). All the N1s bands of AIE-CDs can be deconvoluted into two peaks corresponding to amino Ns (399.4 eV) and pyrrolic Ns (400.1 eV). Notably, the C1s and N1s spectra of the B-AIE-CDs were different from those of the other three CDs. In contrast to the peaks of G-AIE-CDs, Y-AIE-CDs, and O-AIE-CDs, new peaks of carboxylic CO (287.8 eV) and graphitic Ns (287.8 eV) were observed for B-AIE-CDs (Table 2 ). This may be the reason for the colour of the B-AIE-CDs being different to the other three coloured CDs. As seen in Table 2, with the increase in the content of sulfuric acid, the content of S-doping in G-AIE-CDs, Y-AIE-CDs, and O-AIE-CDs did not increase, indicating that the content of S did not affect the colour of the AIE-CDs. However, the carbon contents of G-AIE-CDs (86.74%), Y-AIE-CDs (86.14%), and O-AIE-CDs (86.02%) were significantly higher than those of B-AIE-CDs (76.26%). The contents of CC/CC (50.3%, 53.3%, 52.7%, and 52.6% for B-AIE-CDs, G-AIE-CDs, Y-AIE-CDs, and O-AIE-CDs, respectively) and CN/CS (15.8%, 28.7%, 28.7%, and 28.7% for B-AIE-CDs, G-AIE-CDs, Y-AIE-CDs, and O-AIE-CDs, respectively) also increased. These results demonstrate that the addition of sulfuric acid can increase the degree of carbonisation of the prepared-AIE-CDs. Besides, the amount of sulfuric acid also plays an important role in N-doping. The content of nitrogen gradually increased with an increase in the amount of sulfuric acid (nitrogen content for B-AIE-CDs, G-AIE-CDs, Y-AIE-CDs, and O-AIE-CDs was 6.15%, 10.23%, 10.55%, and 10.89%, respectively).The content of amino Ns significantly increased (B-AIE-CDs, G-AIE-CDs, Y-AIE-CDs, and O-AIE-CDs were 1.40%, 7.70%, 8.50%, and 9.00%, respectively), whereas there was little difference in the content of pyrrolic Ns (B-AIE-CDs, G-AIE-CDs, Y-AIE-CDs, and O-AIE-CDs were 1.50%, 2.50%, 2.10%, and 1.90%, respectively). However, in the three S-doped AIE-CDs, the content of pyrrolic Ns gradually decreased with an increase in the amount of sulfuric acid. These results indicate that the introduction of sulfuric acid can greatly promote the degree of carbonisation of the prepared-AIE-CDs and has a significant influence on the type and content of N. With an increase in the content of sulfuric acid, the content of N increased significantly, and the colour of the prepared AIE-CDs experienced a redshift from blue. Moreover, with the increase of amino Ns and the decrease of pyrrolic Ns, the redshift phenomenon appeared more distinct. This further indicates that the amount of sulfuric acid was the main factor affecting the colour of the prepared-AIE-CDs.

Furthermore, the functional groups of the four types of AIE-CDs were analysed using FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 E, all the AIE-CDs contained the same seven stretching–vibration bands in their FT-IR spectra, such as OH and NH at 3420 and 3240 cm−1, CN/CO at 1590 cm−1, CC at 1520 cm−1, CH at 2900 cm−1 and 1390 cm−1, and CN/CO/CS at 1160 cm−1. These results illustrate that during the reaction, abundant chemical groups (such as amino and hydroxyl groups), which were not present in the precursor and sulfuric acid, were formed on the surface of the AIE-CDs. In addition, it is apparent from Fig. 2 E that compared to the FT-IR spectrum of B-AIE-CDs, the peripheral active functional groups (NH/OH) were significantly reduced in the other three types of AIE-CDs; this indicates that the degree of carbonisation increased significantly with the addition of sulfuric acid. Moreover, comparing the FT-IR spectra of G-AIE-CDs, Y-AIE-CDs, and O-AIE-CDs, the peripheral active functional groups increased with a gradual increase in the content of sulfuric acid, which was consistent with the XPS results. Since the reaction temperature and time also affected the colour of the CDs, the FT-IR spectra of the CDs prepared at different temperatures and times were recorded. Based on O-AIE-CDs, CDs were prepared for 6 and 12 h. As shown in Fig. 2 F, with an increase in the reaction time, CO/CN and the degree of carbonisation increased, contributing to the blueshift of the emission wavelength of the CDs (Table 1). Based on O-AIE-CDs, CDs at 220 °C and 240 °C were prepared. As described in Fig. 2 G, with the increase in reaction temperature, the number of peripheral active functional groups increased and the emission wavelength of the CDs blueshifted (Table 1). These results indicate that the reaction time can affect the degree of carbonisation and thus, affect the emission wavelength of the CDs. In addition, the reaction temperature influenced the content of the peripheral active functional groups; therefore, it also affected the emission wavelength of the CDs. In general, the XPS and FT-IR results demonstrated that the amount of sulfuric acid, reaction temperature, and reaction time all affect the emission wavelength of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical performance and AIE properties of AIE-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The UV–vis spectra of crystal violet, B-AIE-CDs, G-AIE-CDs, and O-AIE-CDs were recorded. As shown in Fig. 3
A, the UV–vis spectra of the four types of AIE-CDs were different from that of crystal violet, indicating that the structures of AIE-CDs were different from that of crystal violet. As shown in Fig. 3 B, the UV–vis spectrum of B-AIE-CDs exhibited significant absorption in the UV region, with a peak located at approximately 254 nm, which is frequently attributed to the π-π* electron transition of CC [32]. In addition, the peaks which were attributed to the n-π* transition related to the CS/CN bond, located at approximately 430–520 nm in the UV–vis spectra of B-AIE-CDs, G-AIE-CDs, Y-AIE-CDs, and O-AIE-CDs, were gradually redshifted (Fig. 3 B). These results implied that, compared to B-AIE-CDs, the absorption of the other three AIE-CDs redshifted when their graphite lattice was doped with S. The redshift became more apparent as the content of sulfuric acid used in the reaction solvent increased. These results show that sulfuric acid may have a significant influence on the structure of the CDs. Moreover, it is apparent from Fig. 3 B that the O-AIE-CDs exhibited the widest optical absorption range. B-AIE-CDs, G-AIE-CDs, Y-AIE-CDs, and O-AIE-CDs possessed absorption edges at 457, 570, 590, and 650 nm, respectively. The absorption cut-off edge of the O-AIE-CDs showed a redshift of approximately 193 nm in relation to that of the B-AIE-CDs. It is well-known that a redshift is typically related to the narrowing of the band-gap energy and is often found in long-wavelength emissive CDs [33]. The band-gap energies of AIE-CDs were calculated using the equation Eg opt = 1240/λedge, where λedge is the wavelength of the maximum absorption edge. As described in Fig. 3 C-F, the calculated band-gap energies gradually decreased from 5.48 to 4.61 eV from B-AIE-CDs to O-AIE-CDs, respectively. This result is consistent with the redshift phenomenon of the fluorescence emission wavelength, further confirming the quantum-size effect of AIE-CDs [34]. In addition, the influence of reaction temperature and reaction time on the fluorescence emission wavelengths of the obtained-CDs was also verified using band-gap energies. As shown in Figure S3 , with an increase in the reaction time and temperature, the band-gap energies of the synthesised-CDs increased, and their fluorescence emission wavelengths were blueshifted. These phenomena show that the reaction time and temperature may also influence the fluorescence colour of the prepared-CDs.

Subsequently, the fluorescence properties of the AIE-CDs were investigated. As described in Scheme 1, the colours of B-AIE-CDs, G-AIE-CDs, Y-AIE-CDs, and O-AIE-CDs ranged from colourless to brown under natural light and emitted bright blue-green, green, yellow, and orange fluorescence, respectively, under a 360 nm UV lamp. Moreover, the maximum emission wavelengths of B-AIE-CDs, G-AIE-CDs, Y-AIE-CDs, and O-AIE-CDs were 480, 527, 559, and 580 nm, respectively, at excitation wavelengths of 360, 450, 490, and 500 nm, respectively (Table 1 and Fig. 4
A-D).

The AIE properties of the four types of AIE-CDs were studied. Different concentrations of the four types of AIE-CDs were prepared, and their fluorescence was analysed under sunlight and 365 nm UV lamps. As described in Fig. 5
A-D, with the increase in concentration, the fluorescence of AIE-CDs with four colours gradually strengthened, indicating that aggregation did not quench their fluorescence, which is a typical characteristic of AIE [35]. This phenomenon indicates that the four AIE-CDs had AIE properties. To understand the AIE properties of the synthesised-AIE-CDs, O-AIE-CDs were used as a representative for the study. As seen from Fig. 5 E-G, with increasing concentrations of O-AIE-CDs from 0.01 mg·mL−1 to 1 mg·mL−1, the emission wavelengths of O-AIE-CDs continued to redshift from 521 nm to 580 nm and the band-gap energies decreased (5.63 to 4.62 eV, shown in Figure S4
A-C). These results suggest that the emission of green light near the emission wavelength of 521 nm gradually decreased, which was attributed to aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ), similar to conventional CDs. Simultaneously, the orange emission near the emission wavelength of 580 nm gradually increased owing to the AIE. These observations imply that O-AIE-CDs can be converted from ACQ to AIE and exhibit adjustable fluorescence [36].

The successful preparation of the four kinds of AIE-CDs suggested the feasibility of the strategy for preparing multi-colour-emission fluorescent AIE-CDs. The simultaneous acquisition of AIE-CDs in four colours provided a greater application potential for CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of AIE-CDs for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption

ce:section.ce:para:
Excluding B-AIE-CDs, the other three kinds of AIE-CDs showed a great response to different pH values; therefore, they could be used as anti-counterfeiting agents. Owing to their different fluorescent colours and AIE properties, they can be used as multiple anti-counterfeiting agents. As described in Figure S5
A-C, when the pH = 1, three AIE-CDs (0.1 mg·mL−1) showed the strongest emission intensities. With an increase in pH, the fluorescence of these three AIE-CDs gradually decreased, and almost no fluorescence was observed at pH > 4. This phenomenon is attributed to the protonation of the AIE-CDs. Since the purification of AIE-CDs was achieved by neutralisation with NaOH followed by dialysis, the process was a deprotonation process. Therefore, it can be speculated that protonation of AIE-CDs occurs when a sufficient amount of acid is added, and the degree of protonation affects the fluorescence intensity of the prepared-AIE-CDs. Other studies have also reported similar results [34,37]. Song et al. stated that the typical pKa value for the azo-quinone equilibrium of MO is 3.4. The azo-quinone structures attaching to different chromophores may lead to different pKa values. Therefore, they speculated that their CDs also had quinone structures, which under acidic conditions can emit strong red fluorescence through a high concentration of quinone structures due to the presence of positively charged active hydrogen. Under alkaline conditions, quinone structures are transferred to protonated structures on azo, resulting in fluorescence quenching [34]. It is interesting to note that, as seen in Table 2 , from G-AIE-CDs to O-AIE-CDs, the content of amino Ns in their structures gradually increased from 7.7% to 9.0%, indicating that the structures mentioned above may also exist in the structures of these three AIE-CDs. This may explain the reason for the acid-sensitivity of these AIE-CDs. In addition, owing to the properties of these three AIE-CDs, they can be used for anti-counterfeiting under acidic conditions.

Note that the purified AIE-CDs powders had rich functional groups on their surfaces, which allowed them to adhere well to the filter paper and could be hand-painted to obtain a variety of fine patterns. Therefore, they can be used as fluorescent ink. As seen in Fig. 6
A-B, these patterns were not different under daylight, even when exposed to 365 nm UV light. However, after wetting with sulfuric-acid solution (pH = 1), the real password “SU” showed orange fluorescence under the portable 365 nm UV lamp as they were written using fluorescent ink of O-AIE-CDs powders. However, the false password “WM” showed other colours (yellow and green) as they were written using fluorescent inks of Y-AIE-CDs and G-AIE-CDs powders, respectively (Fig. 6 C-D). Compared to aggregation-induced quenching CDs, AIE-CDs showed strong fluorescence intensity and stability in anti-counterfeiting without the help of external media. Compared to common monochrome UV–visible fluorescent CDs, the fluorescence which can be switched on and off by acids had better anti-counterfeiting ability [13,14]. Hence, the prepared acid-sensitive AIE-CDs may be good candidates as fluorescent inks for fluorescent patterns, printing, anti-counterfeiting, and optoelectronic applications in the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of AIE-CDs for information encryption
ce:section.ce:para: As the AIE-CDs anti-counterfeiting inks were obtained easily, their colours were controlled by changing the synthesis conditions. These characteristics allow AIE-CDs to be used as AIE-CDs fluorescent security inks for building fluorescent security systems. These inks can be used to mask information, which can only be seen under certain conditions. The information cannot be printed or copied, and encrypted messages cannot be forged. For example (Fig. 6 E-H), O-AIE-CDs and Y-AIE-CDsbased anti-counterfeiting inks were used to draw a stick figure of a child flying a kite under a tree. The painting shows no abnormalities under natural or UV light (Fig. 6 E-F). However, after wetting with sulfuric-acid solution (pH = 1), the hidden real information “123456789″ appeared in orange fluorescence under the portable 365 nm UV lamp ( Fig. 6 G-H). Thus, the acid-sensitive AIE-CDs prepared in this study had a high function of information encryption and could act as fluorescent inks without the need for other external media. In addition, due to all three kinds of acid-sensitive AIE-CDs possessing this property, multiple information-encryption fluorescent security systems can be built.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of B-AIE-CDs for rapid cytoplasmic imaging

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the high QY (43.5%) of B-AIE-CDs and its fluorescence being almost unaffected by acid, they may have some potential for cell imaging. The cytotoxicity of B-AIE-CDs in melanoma cells was studied using the MTT assay. As indicated in Figure S6 , B-AIE-CDs did not have significant cytotoxic effects on melanoma cells in the concentration range of 2.5–1000 μg·mL−1, revealing that B-AIE-CDs had little cytotoxicity and could be used for subsequent cell imaging.

Owing to their low toxicity and outstanding AIE fluorescence properties, B-AIE-CDs have been employed for cell imaging. The imaging time of the B-AIE-CDs in melanoma cells was studied. As shown in Fig. 7
A, after treatment with B-AIE-CDs, distinct fluorescence was observed within 3 min, and the fluorescence of the cells gradually increased over time. The imaging effect of B-AIE-CDs on cells was good after 5 min. Thus, an imaging time of 5 min was selected. It is noteworthy that, with the increase in time, the amount of B-AIE-CDs entering the cell gradually increased, and more B-AIE-CDs accumulated and formed aggregates; thus, the AIE characteristics of B-AIE-CDs gradually increased. Therefore, the fluorescence of cells gradually increased with the increase in time from 5 min to 480 min.

Furthermore, the influence of different concentrations of B-AIE-CDs on cell imaging was investigated. As indicated in Fig. 7 B, with an increase in the concentration of B-AIE-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of the melanoma cells increased and reached a sufficient level at 50 μg·mL−1. Therefore, 50 μg·mL−1 was selected as the imaging concentration.

Melanoma cells were co-incubated with B-AIE-CDs, and an organelle commercial dye (DAPI, a fluorescent dye that binds to DNA strongly and is specifically used for nuclear staining) was used to investigate the distribution of B-AIE-CDs in the cells. As shown in Fig. 8
A-C, DAPI stained the central nucleus of the melanoma cell blue, while B-AIE-CDs were mainly distributed in the peripheral cytoplasm. These results demonstrated that the B-AIE-CDs were mainly distributed in the cytoplasm. Therefore, B-AIE-CDs can be used for rapid cytoplasmic imaging with low toxicity, showing great potential as a fluorescent dye for cytoplasmic imaging.

The mechanism of cellular uptake of B-AIE-CDs was also studied. The energy-dependence of cell uptake was studied. As reported, low temperature (4 °C) and NaN3 can interfere with the production of ATP in cells and also block the endocytosis pathway [38,39]. Thus, the cells were pre-treated with NaN3 (10 mM) at low temperature (4 °C) for 4 h and then incubated with 50 μg·mL-1B-AIE-CDs. As shown in Fig. 9
A-C, compared to the control group, the pre-treated cells showed significantly lower fluorescence intensities, revealing that the uptake of cells was energy-dependent.

According to previous studies, cell uptake pathways generally include lipid raft-mediated endocytosis, clathrin-mediated endocytosis, micropinocytosis, and caveolae-mediated endocytosis [40]. Thus, to explore the cell uptake pathway of the B-AIE-CDs, melanoma cells were pre-treated with 10 μg·mL−1 of CPZ (an endocytosis inhibitor of clathrin-mediated endocytosis), 100 μg·mL−1 of genistein (an endocytosis inhibitor of caveolae-mediated endocytosis), 10 μg·mL−1 of MβCD (an endocytosis inhibitor of lipid raft-mediated endocytosis), and 20 μg·mL−1 of amiloride (an endocytosis inhibitor of micropinocytosis) for 4 h. Next, the images were obtained after cells were incubated with 50 μg·mL-1B-AIE-CDs for 5 min. Compared to the control group (Fig. 9 A), the fluorescence intensity of the cells pre-treated with CPZ decreased significantly (Fig. 9 D), whereas minor changes in the fluorescence intensities of the other three groups were observed (Fig. 9 E-G). These phenomena suggest that the cellular uptake pathway of B-AIE-CDs is mainly attributed to clathrin-mediated endocytosis. Thus, the cellular uptake pathway of B-AIE-CDs consumed energy and was attributed to clathrin-mediated endocytosis. The details of this are shown in Scheme 2
.

Compared to previous reports [1,36,40], a novel synthesis strategy (by varying the content of sulfuric acid in the solvent, reaction temperature, and reaction time) for tunable four-coloured fluorescent AIE-CDs is reported for the first time in this study. Similar to other research [41], we found that after the addition of sulfuric acid, the degree of carbonisation of the precursor increased, the size of AIE-CDs increased, the band-gap energy decreased, and the emission wavelength of AIE-CDs was redshifted. Moreover, the results of XPS analysis revealed that the sulfuric acid content also affected the content and type of N in the AIE-CDs. By increasing the sulfuric acid content, pyrrolic Ns was pyrolysed to amino Ns, and the content of pyrrolic Ns decreased. As a result, the emission wavelength of AIE-CDs was redshifted further. In addition, the reaction temperature and time also affected the surface-defect state and the degree of carbonisation of AIE-CDs, affecting the emission wavelength and QY of the AIE-CDs. The discovery of these fluorescence mechanisms provides a reasonable explanation for the changes in the fluorescence behaviour of the AIE-CDs. The development of this synthesis strategy provides a new approach for the preparation of other CDs with long emission wavelengths and high QYs. In addition, the successful preparation of multi-colour acid-sensitive fluorescent AIE-CDs is of great significance for securing on–off anti-counterfeiting, information encryption, and rapid cytoplasmic imaging.

Lijuan Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Shaochi Liu: Data curation, Investigation. Tian Quan: Data curation, Investigation. Yongqing Tao: Software, Investigation. Meng Tian: Software, Investigation. Luchun Wang: Software, Investigation. Junji Wang: Software, Investigation. Dandan Wang: Supervision, Writing – original draft. Die Gao: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21904109) and a joint program of Luzhou Government and Southwest Medical University (2020LZXNYDJ25).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Detailed descriptions of the calculation of QY and some experimental results were supplied as. Supporting Information. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.01.004.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Haynes, Malic acid carbon dots: From super-resolution live-cell imaging to highly efficient separation, ACS Nano 12(6) (2018) 5741-5752. DOI: 10.1021/acsnano.8b01619.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red emissive carbon dots obtained from direct calcination of 1,2,4-triaminobenzene for dual-mode pH sensing in living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.Y. Chen, C. Wang, Y.L. Xu, G.X. Ran, Q.J. Song, Red emissive carbon dots obtained from direct calcination of 1,2,4-triaminobenzene for dual-mode pH sensing in living cells, New. J. Chem. 44(17) (2020) 7210-7217. DOI: 10.1039/d0nj00985g.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Full-color emission polymer carbon dots with quench-resistant solid-state fluorescence

Chen, Solid-state fluorescent carbon dots with aggregation-induced yellow emission for white light-emitting diodes with high luminous efficiencies, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 11(27) (2019) 24395-24403. DOI: 10.1021/acsami.9b04600.

B 4(5) (2016) 834-843. DOI: 10.1039/c5tb02183a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly fluorescent carbon dots from enokitake mushroom as multi-faceted optical nanomaterials for Cr6+ and VOC detection and imaging applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.R. Pacquiao, M.D.G. de Luna, N. Thongsai, S. Kladsomboon, P. Paoprasert, Highly fluorescent carbon dots from enokitake mushroom as multi-faceted optical nanomaterials for Cr6+ and VOC detection and imaging applications, Appl. Surf. Sci. 453 (2018) 192-203. DOI: 10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.04.199.
